message.content:
Advance plan-flow goal **G13** (on the `goals` ledger, milestone M50) by exactly ONE state-driven step in default SINGLE-planner mode.

G13 is a freshly bootstrapped goal in `clarifying` status with no questions filed yet. Perform the appropriate first step: read the goal's current state and file the FIRST batch of clarifying questions, then return `awaiting-answers`.

Context you should be aware of (do not re-ask what is already settled):
- This is the wanbond/fecalia multipath WireGuard project. The goal concerns operability gaps found empirically while testing `policy = "weighted"` with pacing on and off.
- A RELATED goal **G2** is already `planned` (milestones M13–M17) and covers broad pacing empirical BDP sizing (Q20: wire SizePacingFromBDP from declared per-link bandwidth + document) and validates enabled pacing does NOT starve WireGuard control frames. G13 is operability-specific and adds: (i) a `wanbond_aggregation_engaged` metric + EWMA-offered-load-vs-threshold observability; (ii) a weighted-mode capacity-sanity WARN/FAIL-LOUD guard tied to install.md §3a; (iii) a latency/priority class so control/probe/ICMP frames aren't dropped indiscriminately under pacer overload. Where item 2's auto-derive or item 3(a)'s sizing docs would duplicate G2, your questions should probe the boundary against G2 rather than re-planning it.
- The deliverable requires acceptance criteria per task, each reproducible on a bandwidth-capped netns fixture (no metered WAN needed). The repo has TestFixtureImpairment (test/e2e/fixture_impairment_test.go, T35) as such a fixture.

Read G13's full description from the ledger for the complete goal text. File your first clarifying questions against the `questions` ledger, linked to `goals:G13`, and transition the goal appropriately. End your reply with a `### Session summary` section (goal id, role: planner, returned status token, the questions you filed).

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

```go
477	// Path is one physical WAN uplink. The edge binds each path's UDP socket to
478	// SourceAddr so the upstream router pins it to the intended WAN; the
479	// concentrator learns the real per-path endpoints from authenticated traffic.
480	type Path struct {

482		Name string `toml:"name"`

484		SourceAddr netip.Addr `toml:"-"`
485		// SourceAddrRaw is the TOML string form of SourceAddr; parsed in normalize.
486		SourceAddrRaw string `toml:"source_addr"`
487		// DestAddr is the OPTIONAL per-path concentrator endpoint ("ip:port") the
488		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
489		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
490		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
491		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
492		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.
493		DestAddr netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
494		// DestAddrRaw is the TOML string form of DestAddr; parsed in normalize.
495		DestAddrRaw string `toml:"dest_addr"`
496		// LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec is the OPERATOR-DECLARED bottleneck bandwidth of this
497		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to
498		// size the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the bandwidth-delay product
499		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
500		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.
501		// Zero means "not declared" (the synthetic default pace is kept).
502		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
503		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.
504		// "50Mbit" / "1Gbit" / "500kbit" (SI bit/s units; the "bit" suffix may be written
505		// "bps"). Parsed in normalize; a non-positive or unparseable value fails fast.
506		LinkBandwidthRaw string `toml:"link_bandwidth"`
507		// LinkRTT is the OPERATOR-DECLARED baseline RTT of this uplink, parsed from
508		// LinkRTTRaw in normalize. It is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product pace
509		// burst (one RTT of in-flight frames); required (> 0) when LinkBandwidth is set and
510		// pacing is enabled under the weighted policy, ignored otherwise.
511		LinkRTT time.Duration `toml:"-"`
512		// LinkRTTRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LinkRTT, e.g. "45ms". Parsed in
513		// normalize; an unparseable or non-positive value fails fast.
```

```go
41	// the outer-seq is connection-global — a single sequence the send scheduler

45	// loss is a separate, correctly-scoped metric: see ConnLoss.

48	// owner (Prober) serializes access. It is exercised directly on synthetic traces.

100	// lossWindow is a sliding-window loss estimator over a monotonic sequence space.
101	// It tracks, for the trailing win sequence numbers ending at the highest seen,
102	// which were received; loss is the unfilled fraction of that window. It never
103	// inspects payloads — only sequence presence — so a burst of missing sequence
104	// numbers reads as loss without any per-packet state beyond the ring.
105	//
106	// The window is bounded below by the FIRST observed sequence, not by zero, so a
107	// stream that starts (or a receiver that attaches) mid-sequence does not count
108	// the never-seen prefix as loss.
109	type lossWindow struct {
110		win     int
111		recv    []bool
112		first   uint64
113		highest uint64
114		have    bool
115	}
116	
117	func newLossWindow(win int) *lossWindow {
118		if win <= 0 {
119			win = defaultLossWindow
120		}
121		return &lossWindow{win: win, recv: make([]bool, win)}
122	}
123	
124	func (w *lossWindow) slot(seq uint64) int { return int(seq % uint64(w.win)) }
125	
126	func (w *lossWindow) observe(seq uint64) {
127		if !w.have {
128			w.have = true
129			w.first = seq
130			w.highest = seq
131			for i := range w.recv {
132				w.recv[i] = false
133			}
134			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
135			return
136		}
137		if seq > w.highest {
138			// The positions (highest, seq] are newly exposed by the window advancing;
139			// mark them missing, then record the one that actually arrived.
140			if seq-w.highest >= uint64(w.win) {
141				for i := range w.recv {
142					w.recv[i] = false
143				}
144			} else {
145				for k := w.highest + 1; k <= seq; k++ {
146					w.recv[w.slot(k)] = false
147				}
148			}
149			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
150			w.highest = seq
151			return
152		}
153		// Late/reordered arrival: fill its slot only if it still falls in the window.
154		if w.highest-seq < uint64(w.win) {
155			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
156		}
157	}
158	
159	func (w *lossWindow) fraction() float64 {
160		if !w.have {
161			return 0
162		}
163		lower := uint64(0)
164		if w.highest+1 > uint64(w.win) {
165			lower = w.highest + 1 - uint64(w.win)
166		}
167		if lower < w.first {
168			lower = w.first // never charge loss for sequences before the first observed
169		}
170		n := w.highest - lower + 1
171		missing := 0
172		for k := lower; k <= w.highest; k++ {
173			if !w.recv[w.slot(k)] {
174				missing++
175			}
176		}
177		return float64(missing) / float64(n)
178	}
179	
180	// ConnLoss estimates CONNECTION-SCOPED loss from the connection-global outer-seq
181	// DATA stream. The outer-seq is a single sequence the send scheduler stripes
182	// across every path (T12) and the receiver resequences into one global order
183	// (T18), so this is explicitly NOT a per-path metric: feeding it a single path's
184	// frames would read scheduler striping as loss. Feed it EVERY received DATA
185	// frame's OuterSeq regardless of which path delivered it. For per-path loss use
186	// Estimator/Prober, which measures the active probe stream instead.
187	//
188	// ConnLoss is safe for concurrent use by the per-path receive goroutines.
189	type ConnLoss struct {
190		mu sync.Mutex
191		w  *lossWindow
192	}
193	
194	// NewConnLoss builds a ConnLoss over a trailing window of window outer-sequence
195	// numbers (defaultLossWindow when non-positive).
196	func NewConnLoss(window int) *ConnLoss {
197		return &ConnLoss{w: newLossWindow(window)}
198	}
199

201	// loss estimate.
202	func (c *ConnLoss) Observe(outerSeq uint64) {
203		c.mu.Lock()
204		defer c.mu.Unlock()
205		c.w.observe(outerSeq)
206	}
207	
208	// Loss returns the current connection loss fraction in [0,1].
209	func (c *ConnLoss) Loss() float64 {
210		c.mu.Lock()
211		defer c.mu.Unlock()
212		return c.w.fraction()
213	}
```

```go
59	func NewEstimator(lossWindow int) *Estimator {
60		return &Estimator{loss: newLossWindow(lossWindow)}
61	}
62	
63	// ObserveRTT folds one round-trip-time sample into the smoothed RTT and jitter.
64	// The first sample seeds srtt directly and rttvar at half the sample, per RFC
65	// 6298.
66	func (e *Estimator) ObserveRTT(sample time.Duration) {
67		s := float64(sample)
68		if !e.haveRTT {
69			e.srtt = s
70			e.rttvar = s / 2
71			e.haveRTT = true
72			return
73		}
74		d := e.srtt - s
75		if d < 0 {
76			d = -d
77		}
78		e.rttvar = (1-rttBeta)*e.rttvar + rttBeta*d
79		e.srtt = (1-rttAlpha)*e.srtt + rttAlpha*s
80	}
81	
82	// ObserveProbeEcho folds one received probe echo's ProbeSeq into the per-path
83	// loss estimate. The prober assigns ProbeSeq densely (0,1,2,…) on this path, so a
84	// gap in received echo seqs is a probe that did not round-trip — i.e. per-path
85	// loss. Echoes may arrive out of order; a late arrival within the window
86	// retroactively fills its gap, and a duplicate is idempotent.
87	func (e *Estimator) ObserveProbeEcho(seq uint64) {
88		e.loss.observe(seq)
89	}
90	
91	// Estimate returns the current fused snapshot.
92	func (e *Estimator) Estimate() Estimate {
93		return Estimate{
94			RTT:    time.Duration(e.srtt),
95			Jitter: time.Duration(e.rttvar),
96			Loss:   e.loss.fraction(),
97		}
98	}
99	
100	// lossWindow is a sliding-window loss estimator over a monotonic sequence space.
101	// It tracks, for the trailing win sequence numbers ending at the highest seen,
102	// which were received; loss is the unfilled fraction of that window. It never
103	// inspects payloads — only sequence presence — so a burst of missing sequence
104	// numbers reads as loss without any per-packet state beyond the ring.
105	//
106	// The window is bounded below by the FIRST observed sequence, not by zero, so a
107	// stream that starts (or a receiver that attaches) mid-sequence does not count
108	// the never-seen prefix as loss.
109	type lossWindow struct {
110		win     int
111		recv    []bool
112		first   uint64
113		highest uint64
114		have    bool
115	}
116	
117	func newLossWindow(win int) *lossWindow {
118		if win <= 0 {
119			win = defaultLossWindow
120		}
121		return &lossWindow{win: win, recv: make([]bool, win)}
122	}
123	
124	func (w *lossWindow) slot(seq uint64) int { return int(seq % uint64(w.win)) }
125	
126	func (w *lossWindow) observe(seq uint64) {
127		if !w.have {
128			w.have = true
129			w.first = seq
130			w.highest = seq
131			for i := range w.recv {
132				w.recv[i] = false
133			}
134			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
135			return
136		}
137		if seq > w.highest {
138			// The positions (highest, seq] are newly exposed by the window advancing;
139			// mark them missing, then record the one that actually arrived.
140			if seq-w.highest >= uint64(w.win) {
141				for i := range w.recv {
142					w.recv[i] = false
143				}
144			} else {
145				for k := w.highest + 1; k <= seq; k++ {
146					w.recv[w.slot(k)] = false
147				}
148			}
149			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
150			w.highest = seq
151			return
152		}
153		// Late/reordered arrival: fill its slot only if it still falls in the window.
154		if w.highest-seq < uint64(w.win) {
155			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
156		}
157	}
158	
159	func (w *lossWindow) fraction() float64 {
160		if !w.have {
161			return 0
162		}
163		lower := uint64(0)
164		if w.highest+1 > uint64(w.win) {
165			lower = w.highest + 1 - uint64(w.win)
166		}
167		if lower < w.first {
168			lower = w.first // never charge loss for sequences before the first observed
169		}
170		n := w.highest - lower + 1
171		missing := 0
172		for k := lower; k <= w.highest; k++ {
173			if !w.recv[w.slot(k)] {
174				missing++
175			}
176		}
177		return float64(missing) / float64(n)
178	}
179	
180	// ConnLoss estimates CONNECTION-SCOPED loss from the connection-global outer-seq
181	// DATA stream. The outer-seq is a single sequence the send scheduler stripes
182	// across every path (T12) and the receiver resequences into one global order
183	// (T18), so this is explicitly NOT a per-path metric: feeding it a single path's
184	// frames would read scheduler striping as loss. Feed it EVERY received DATA
185	// frame's OuterSeq regardless of which path delivered it. For per-path loss use
186	// Estimator/Prober, which measures the active probe stream instead.
187	//
188	// ConnLoss is safe for concurrent use by the per-path receive goroutines.
189	type ConnLoss struct {
190		mu sync.Mutex
191		w  *lossWindow
192	}
193	
194	// NewConnLoss builds a ConnLoss over a trailing window of window outer-sequence
195	// numbers (defaultLossWindow when non-positive).
196	func NewConnLoss(window int) *ConnLoss {
197		return &ConnLoss{w: newLossWindow(window)}
198	}
199	
200	// Observe folds one received DATA frame's connection-global OuterSeq into the
201	// loss estimate.
202	func (c *ConnLoss) Observe(outerSeq uint64) {
203		c.mu.Lock()
204		defer c.mu.Unlock()
205		c.w.observe(outerSeq)
206	}
207	
208	// Loss returns the current connection loss fraction in [0,1].
209	func (c *ConnLoss) Loss() float64 {
210		c.mu.Lock()
211		defer c.mu.Unlock()
212		return c.w.fraction()
213	}
```

```go
183	// NewWeighted builds the weighted-aggregation scheduler over parallel health and
184	// quality slices in priority order (index 0 = preferred primary). health and quality
185	// must be the same length; a quality element MAY be nil (that path then gets a
186	// neutral weight until a real PathQuality is wired). It fails fast on an empty or
187	// length-mismatched set, a nil health element, a nil dependency, or an invalid config.
188	func NewWeighted(health []PathHealth, quality []PathQuality, cfg WeightedConfig, clock telemetry.Clock, logger log.Logger) (*WeightedScheduler, error) {
189		if len(health) == 0 {
190			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
191		}
192		if len(quality) != len(health) {
193			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: quality set (%d) must be one entry per health source (%d)", len(quality), len(health))
194		}
195		for i, h := range health {
196			if h == nil {
197				return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: path %d health source is nil", i)
198			}
199		}
200		if clock == nil {
201			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: clock is required")
202		}
203		if logger == nil {
204			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: logger is required")
205		}
206		if err := cfg.validate(); err != nil {
207			return nil, err
208		}
209		return &WeightedScheduler{
210			health:  append([]PathHealth(nil), health...),
211			quality: append([]PathQuality(nil), quality...),
212			clock:   clock,
213			cfg:     cfg,
214			log:     logger.Component("sched"),
215			current: make([]float64, len(health)),
216			tokens:  fullBuckets(len(health), cfg.PacingBurst),
217			active:  -1,
218		}, nil
219	}

221	// SetFEC installs the P3+ redundancy policy (nil clears it). It is the documented
222	// extension point for FEC-aware path selection; the current single-path Pick does
223	// not consult it (redundant transmission needs the P3 Bind fan-out), but
224	// RedundantPaths exposes it so a P3 Bind can query the additional paths per frame.
225	func (s *WeightedScheduler) SetFEC(fec FECPolicy) {
226		s.mu.Lock()
227		defer s.mu.Unlock()
228		s.fec = fec
229	}
230	
231	// RedundantPaths reports the ADDITIONAL path indices a frame scheduled onto chosen
232	// should also egress on, per the installed FECPolicy over the current eligible set.
233	// It returns nil when no policy is installed (no FEC). It is the P3 hook a redundant-
234	// transmission Bind would call alongside Pick; today it is a documented, unit-tested
235	// seam with no in-tree caller on the hot path.
236	func (s *WeightedScheduler) RedundantPaths(chosen int) []int {
237		s.mu.Lock()
238		defer s.mu.Unlock()
239		if s.fec == nil {
240			return nil
241		}
242		return s.fec.RedundantPaths(chosen, s.eligibleLocked())
243	}
244	
245	// Pick selects the path for the NEXT frame and advances all per-frame distribution
246	// state (offered-load meter, aggregation gate, pacing buckets, round-robin credits).
247	// It returns a negative value when no path is eligible OR when every eligible path
248	// is paced out (the frame is dropped rather than queued — pacing bounds egress and,
249	// by dropping the overflow, bounds the send backlog). It is safe for concurrent
250	// callers.
251	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Pick(class FrameClass) int {
252		now := s.clock.Now()
253		s.mu.Lock()
254		defer s.mu.Unlock()
255	
256		// Capture the inter-sample idle gap BEFORE observeLoadLocked advances lastLoad: a
257		// long gap since the previous offered frame is itself the strongest evidence of low
258		// load, and the collapse hysteresis must count it (criticism #1 — otherwise an
259		// abrupt overload stop leaves the gate engaged across the whole idle span and the
260		// next low burst dribbles onto the metered path).
261		gap := s.loadGapLocked(now)
262		s.recomputeLocked(now)       // liveness refresh + eager-failover log
263		s.observeLoadLocked(now)     // this Pick is one offered frame (decays across the gap)
264		s.updateGateLocked(now, gap) // engage/disengage aggregation (hysteresis, idle-aware)
265		s.refillLocked(now)          // top up pacing buckets
266	
267		eligible := s.eligibleLocked()
268		if len(eligible) == 0 {
269			return PickNone
270		}
271		if !s.aggregating {
272			// Data-thrift: at low load only the primary (best eligible) carries traffic;
273			// the metered backup stays idle. A path-down excludes it from eligible, so
274			// eligible[0] is the surviving highest-priority path — failover is preserved.
275			return s.serveLocked(now, eligible[0], class)
276		}
277		weights := s.weightsLocked(eligible)
278		return s.selectAggregatingLocked(now, eligible, weights, class)
279	}
280	
281	// loadGapLocked returns the wall-clock gap since the previous offered-load sample, or
282	// 0 before the first sample. Caller holds s.mu.
283	func (s *WeightedScheduler) loadGapLocked(now time.Time) time.Duration {
284		if !s.haveLoad {
285			return 0
286		}
287		gap := now.Sub(s.lastLoad)
288		if gap < 0 {
289			gap = 0
290		}
291		return gap
292	}
293	
294	// Recompute refreshes only the liveness-derived eligible/active set from the current
295	// PathHealth and logs any active-path transition. It advances NONE of the per-frame
296	// distribution state (load meter, gate, pacing, round-robin credits), so the eager-
297	// failover nudge (defect D18/T40) drives an egress-lull failover recompute through it
298	// WITHOUT stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. It is
299	// safe for concurrent callers.
300	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Recompute() {
301		now := s.clock.Now()
302		s.mu.Lock()
303		defer s.mu.Unlock()
304		s.recomputeLocked(now)
305	}
306	
307	// recomputeLocked refreshes the active-primary cache (best eligible path) and logs a
308	// transition on change. Caller holds s.mu.
309	func (s *WeightedScheduler) recomputeLocked(_ time.Time) {
310		best := s.bestEligibleLocked()
311		s.setActiveLocked(best)
312	}
313	
314	// serveLocked applies pacing to a single chosen path: it returns idx when the frame
315	// is admitted, or PickPaced when pacing is on and the path has no token (the frame is
316	// shed — dropped, bounding egress and backlog — and the shedding diagnostic is
317	// rate-limited). A ClassControl frame is pacing-EXEMPT (defect D22): it egresses on
318	// idx unconditionally, spending no token and never shed, so bulk overload cannot
319	// starve WireGuard rekey. Caller holds s.mu.
320	func (s *WeightedScheduler) serveLocked(now time.Time, idx int, class FrameClass) int {
321		if class == ClassControl {
322			return idx
323		}
324		if s.tryConsumeLocked(idx) {
325			return idx
326		}
327		return s.shedLocked(now)
328	}
```

```go
1	package adaptivefec
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"math"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// MaxShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec
10	// (maxShards) and restated in internal/config (maxFECShards): a coding group
11	// carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It is mirrored
12	// here — with this cross-reference rather than an import — so the controller can
13	// fail-fast on an out-of-field parity bound without coupling to the datapath.
14	const MaxShards = 256
15	
16	// Documented default control-law constants. They are the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH
17	// for the tuning the simulation harness exercises; DefaultConfig assembles them.
18	// Every value is named and justified rather than appearing as a magic number in
19	// the algorithm.
20	const (
21		// DefaultDataShards is K, the fixed number of data shards per coding group the
22		// controller sizes parity for. Chosen to match a typical FEC group.
23		DefaultDataShards = 10
24	
25		// DefaultMaxParity caps M at 100% overhead (M == K): beyond a 1:1 parity ratio
26		// the marginal recovery per byte of overhead is poor, so the loop saturates
27		// there rather than spending unbounded bandwidth under pathological loss.
28		DefaultMaxParity = 10
29	
30		// DefaultAlpha is the EWMA weight of the newest loss sample. 0.3 keeps ~3-4
31		// samples of memory: heavy enough to reject single-sample telemetry noise, light
32		// enough to react to a genuine sustained shift within a few probe intervals.
33		DefaultAlpha = 0.3
34	
35		// DefaultRaiseThreshold is the smoothed-loss level (5%) at/above which the loop
36		// starts adding parity. Below it, occasional loss is cheaper to absorb via
37		// retransmit/latency than to mask with standing redundancy.
38		DefaultRaiseThreshold = 0.05
39	
40		// DefaultLowerThreshold is the smoothed-loss level (2%) at/below which the loop
41		// is eligible to shed parity. The gap to RaiseThreshold is the hysteresis
42		// deadband: loss wandering between 2% and 5% neither raises nor lowers M.
43		DefaultLowerThreshold = 0.02
44	
45		// DefaultSafetyFactor is the headroom over the mean loss the parity is sized to
46		// mask. 1.5 sizes each group to tolerate 1.5x the smoothed loss, covering the
47		// binomial variance by which an individual group exceeds the mean. It applies
48		// only when TargetResidual is unset (the legacy SafetyFactor sizing mode).
49		DefaultSafetyFactor = 1.5
50	
51		// DefaultMaxStep bounds |ΔM| per RateInterval. A single spike can move M by at
52		// most 2 parity shards per interval, so redundancy slews rather than jumps.
53		DefaultMaxStep = 2
54	
55		// DefaultRateInterval is the minimum wall time between two M changes. Combined
56		// with MaxStep it caps the redundancy slew rate at MaxStep shards per 500ms.
57		DefaultRateInterval = 500 * time.Millisecond
58	
59		// DefaultDwell is how long smoothed loss must stay at/below LowerThreshold before
60		// the first decrease. 3s of quiet is required to shed parity, so a brief lull in
61		// a lossy period does not prematurely strip protection (raise fast, lower slow).
62		DefaultDwell = 3 * time.Second
63	)
64	
65	// Config parameterizes the adaptive FEC controller. All fields are validated at
66	// construction (fail-fast): a mis-tuned band or an out-of-field parity bound is
67	// rejected by NewController rather than producing undefined control behavior at
68	// runtime.
69	type Config struct {
70		// DataShards is K, the fixed group size the controller sizes parity for. >= 1.
71		DataShards int
72		// MaxParity is the upper bound on the emitted M. >= 1 and DataShards+MaxParity
73		// <= MaxShards (the Reed-Solomon field limit).
74		MaxParity int
75		// Alpha is the EWMA weight of the newest loss sample, in (0,1]. Smaller = more
76		// smoothing.
77		Alpha float64
78		// RaiseThreshold is the smoothed-loss level at/above which M may rise. In
79		// (LowerThreshold, 1).
80		RaiseThreshold float64
81		// LowerThreshold is the smoothed-loss level at/below which M may fall. In
82		// [0, RaiseThreshold). The gap to RaiseThreshold is the hysteresis deadband.
83		LowerThreshold float64
84		// SafetyFactor is the headroom multiplier on the smoothed loss the parity is
85		// sized to mask. >= 1 in the SafetyFactor sizing mode (TargetResidual unset);
86		// MUST be 0 when TargetResidual is set (the two modes are mutually exclusive).
87		SafetyFactor float64
88		// TargetResidual, when set (> 0), selects the RESIDUAL-SLA sizing mode and is
89		// the PRIMARY parity surface: instead of the bare SafetyFactor multiplier the
90		// controller derives M by inverting the binomial residual model
91		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss), see residual.go) to the smallest
92		// M whose modeled residual is <= TargetResidual, clamped to [0, MaxParity]. It
93		// SUPERSEDES SafetyFactor (which must be 0 when this is set). Must be in (0,1)
94		// when set; 0 selects the legacy SafetyFactor path.
95		TargetResidual float64
96		// MaxStep bounds |ΔM| applied per RateInterval. >= 1.
97		MaxStep int
98		// RateInterval is the minimum time between two M changes. > 0.
99		RateInterval time.Duration
100		// Dwell is the minimum time smoothed loss must stay at/below LowerThreshold
101		// before the first decrease. >= 0.
102		Dwell time.Duration
103	}
104	
105	// DefaultConfig returns a Config populated from the documented default control
106	// constants.
107	func DefaultConfig() Config {
108		return Config{
109			DataShards:     DefaultDataShards,
110			MaxParity:      DefaultMaxParity,
111			Alpha:          DefaultAlpha,
112			RaiseThreshold: DefaultRaiseThreshold,
113			LowerThreshold: DefaultLowerThreshold,
114			SafetyFactor:   DefaultSafetyFactor,
115			MaxStep:        DefaultMaxStep,
116			RateInterval:   DefaultRateInterval,
117			Dwell:          DefaultDwell,
118		}
119	}
120	
121	// Validate enforces the control-law invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned
122	// controller is rejected at construction rather than misbehaving at runtime.
123	func (c Config) Validate() error {
124		if c.DataShards < 1 {
125			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: DataShards (K) must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
126		}
127		if c.MaxParity < 1 {
128			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: MaxParity must be >= 1, got %d", c.MaxParity)
129		}
130		if c.DataShards+c.MaxParity > MaxShards {
131			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: DataShards+MaxParity must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", MaxShards, c.DataShards+c.MaxParity)
132		}
133		if !(c.Alpha > 0 && c.Alpha <= 1) || math.IsNaN(c.Alpha) {
134			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: Alpha must be in (0,1], got %g", c.Alpha)
135		}
136		if math.IsNaN(c.LowerThreshold) || c.LowerThreshold < 0 {
137			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: LowerThreshold must be >= 0, got %g", c.LowerThreshold)
138		}
139		if math.IsNaN(c.RaiseThreshold) || c.RaiseThreshold >= 1 {
140			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: RaiseThreshold must be < 1, got %g", c.RaiseThreshold)
141		}
142		if c.LowerThreshold >= c.RaiseThreshold {
143			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: LowerThreshold (%g) must be < RaiseThreshold (%g) so the hysteresis deadband is non-empty", c.LowerThreshold, c.RaiseThreshold)
144		}
145		if math.IsNaN(c.TargetResidual) {
146			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: TargetResidual must be a number, got NaN")
147		}
148		if c.TargetResidual != 0 {
149			// Residual-SLA sizing mode: TargetResidual governs and SafetyFactor is inert,
150			// so the two must not both be set (mutually exclusive, fail-fast).
151			if c.TargetResidual < 0 || c.TargetResidual >= 1 {
152				return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: TargetResidual must be in (0,1) when set, got %g", c.TargetResidual)
153			}
154			if c.SafetyFactor != 0 {
155				return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: SafetyFactor and TargetResidual are mutually exclusive (TargetResidual is the primary residual-SLA surface); leave SafetyFactor 0 when TargetResidual is set, got SafetyFactor=%g", c.SafetyFactor)
156			}
157		} else if math.IsNaN(c.SafetyFactor) || math.IsInf(c.SafetyFactor, 0) || c.SafetyFactor < 1 {
158			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: SafetyFactor must be a finite value >= 1 (or set TargetResidual instead), got %g", c.SafetyFactor)
159		}
160		if c.MaxStep < 1 {
161			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: MaxStep must be >= 1, got %d", c.MaxStep)
162		}
163		if c.RateInterval <= 0 {
164			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: RateInterval must be > 0, got %s", c.RateInterval)
165		}
166		if c.Dwell < 0 {
167			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: Dwell must be >= 0, got %s", c.Dwell)
168		}
169		return nil
170	}
```

```go
250	func SizePacingFromBDP(bandwidthBitsPerSec float64, rtt time.Duration, avgWireFrameBytes float64) (BDPSizing, error) {
251		if bandwidthBitsPerSec <= 0 {
252			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing bandwidth must be > 0 bit/s, got %g", bandwidthBitsPerSec)
253		}
254		if rtt <= 0 {
255			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing RTT must be > 0, got %s", rtt)
256		}
257		if avgWireFrameBytes <= 0 {
258			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing average wire frame size must be > 0 bytes, got %g", avgWireFrameBytes)
259		}
260		const bitsPerByte = 8.0
261		capacityFPS := bandwidthBitsPerSec / (bitsPerByte * avgWireFrameBytes)
262		burstFrames := capacityFPS * rtt.Seconds()
263		return BDPSizing{CapacityFPS: capacityFPS, BurstFrames: burstFrames}, nil
264	}
```

```go
183	// NewWeighted builds the weighted-aggregation scheduler over parallel health and
184	// quality slices in priority order (index 0 = preferred primary). health and quality
185	// must be the same length; a quality element MAY be nil (that path then gets a
186	// neutral weight until a real PathQuality is wired). It fails fast on an empty or
187	// length-mismatched set, a nil health element, a nil dependency, or an invalid config.
188	func NewWeighted(health []PathHealth, quality []PathQuality, cfg WeightedConfig, clock telemetry.Clock, logger log.Logger) (*WeightedScheduler, error) {
189		if len(health) == 0 {
190			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
191		}
192		if len(quality) != len(health) {
193			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: quality set (%d) must be one entry per health source (%d)", len(quality), len(health))
194		}
195		for i, h := range health {
196			if h == nil {
197				return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: path %d health source is nil", i)
198			}
199		}
200		if clock == nil {
201			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: clock is required")
202		}
203		if logger == nil {
204			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: logger is required")
205		}
206		if err := cfg.validate(); err != nil {
207			return nil, err
208		}
209		return &WeightedScheduler{
210			health:  append([]PathHealth(nil), health...),
211			quality: append([]PathQuality(nil), quality...),
212			clock:   clock,
213			cfg:     cfg,
214			log:     logger.Component("sched"),
215			current: make([]float64, len(health)),
216			tokens:  fullBuckets(len(health), cfg.PacingBurst),
217			active:  -1,
218		}, nil
219	}

221	// SetFEC installs the P3+ redundancy policy (nil clears it). It is the documented
222	// extension point for FEC-aware path selection; the current single-path Pick does
223	// not consult it (redundant transmission needs the P3 Bind fan-out), but
224	// RedundantPaths exposes it so a P3 Bind can query the additional paths per frame.
225	func (s *WeightedScheduler) SetFEC(fec FECPolicy) {
226		s.mu.Lock()
227		defer s.mu.Unlock()
228		s.fec = fec
229	}
230	
231	// RedundantPaths reports the ADDITIONAL path indices a frame scheduled onto chosen
232	// should also egress on, per the installed FECPolicy over the current eligible set.
233	// It returns nil when no policy is installed (no FEC). It is the P3 hook a redundant-
234	// transmission Bind would call alongside Pick; today it is a documented, unit-tested
235	// seam with no in-tree caller on the hot path.
236	func (s *WeightedScheduler) RedundantPaths(chosen int) []int {
237		s.mu.Lock()
238		defer s.mu.Unlock()
239		if s.fec == nil {
240			return nil
241		}
242		return s.fec.RedundantPaths(chosen, s.eligibleLocked())
243	}
244	
245	// Pick selects the path for the NEXT frame and advances all per-frame distribution
246	// state (offered-load meter, aggregation gate, pacing buckets, round-robin credits).
247	// It returns a negative value when no path is eligible OR when every eligible path
248	// is paced out (the frame is dropped rather than queued — pacing bounds egress and,
249	// by dropping the overflow, bounds the send backlog). It is safe for concurrent
250	// callers.
251	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Pick(class FrameClass) int {
252		now := s.clock.Now()
253		s.mu.Lock()
254		defer s.mu.Unlock()
255	
256		// Capture the inter-sample idle gap BEFORE observeLoadLocked advances lastLoad: a
257		// long gap since the previous offered frame is itself the strongest evidence of low
258		// load, and the collapse hysteresis must count it (criticism #1 — otherwise an
259		// abrupt overload stop leaves the gate engaged across the whole idle span and the
260		// next low burst dribbles onto the metered path).
261		gap := s.loadGapLocked(now)
262		s.recomputeLocked(now)       // liveness refresh + eager-failover log
263		s.observeLoadLocked(now)     // this Pick is one offered frame (decays across the gap)
264		s.updateGateLocked(now, gap) // engage/disengage aggregation (hysteresis, idle-aware)
265		s.refillLocked(now)          // top up pacing buckets
266	
267		eligible := s.eligibleLocked()
268		if len(eligible) == 0 {
269			return PickNone
270		}
271		if !s.aggregating {
272			// Data-thrift: at low load only the primary (best eligible) carries traffic;
273			// the metered backup stays idle. A path-down excludes it from eligible, so
274			// eligible[0] is the surviving highest-priority path — failover is preserved.
275			return s.serveLocked(now, eligible[0], class)
276		}
277		weights := s.weightsLocked(eligible)
278		return s.selectAggregatingLocked(now, eligible, weights, class)
279	}
280	
281	// loadGapLocked returns the wall-clock gap since the previous offered-load sample, or
282	// 0 before the first sample. Caller holds s.mu.
283	func (s *WeightedScheduler) loadGapLocked(now time.Time) time.Duration {
284		if !s.haveLoad {
285			return 0
286		}
287		gap := now.Sub(s.lastLoad)
288		if gap < 0 {
289			gap = 0
290		}
291		return gap
292	}
293	
294	// Recompute refreshes only the liveness-derived eligible/active set from the current
295	// PathHealth and logs any active-path transition. It advances NONE of the per-frame
296	// distribution state (load meter, gate, pacing, round-robin credits), so the eager-
297	// failover nudge (defect D18/T40) drives an egress-lull failover recompute through it
298	// WITHOUT stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. It is
299	// safe for concurrent callers.
300	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Recompute() {
301		now := s.clock.Now()
302		s.mu.Lock()
303		defer s.mu.Unlock()
304		s.recomputeLocked(now)
305	}
306	
307	// recomputeLocked refreshes the active-primary cache (best eligible path) and logs a
308	// transition on change. Caller holds s.mu.
309	func (s *WeightedScheduler) recomputeLocked(_ time.Time) {
310		best := s.bestEligibleLocked()
311		s.setActiveLocked(best)
312	}
313	
314	// serveLocked applies pacing to a single chosen path: it returns idx when the frame
315	// is admitted, or PickPaced when pacing is on and the path has no token (the frame is
316	// shed — dropped, bounding egress and backlog — and the shedding diagnostic is
317	// rate-limited). A ClassControl frame is pacing-EXEMPT (defect D22): it egresses on
318	// idx unconditionally, spending no token and never shed, so bulk overload cannot
319	// starve WireGuard rekey. Caller holds s.mu.
320	func (s *WeightedScheduler) serveLocked(now time.Time, idx int, class FrameClass) int {
321		if class == ClassControl {
322			return idx
323		}
324		if s.tryConsumeLocked(idx) {
325			return idx
326		}
327		return s.shedLocked(now)
328	}
```

```go
1	package adaptivefec
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"math"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// MaxShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec
10	// (maxShards) and restated in internal/config (maxFECShards): a coding group
11	// carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It is mirrored
12	// here — with this cross-reference rather than an import — so the controller can
13	// fail-fast on an out-of-field parity bound without coupling to the datapath.
14	const MaxShards = 256
15	
16	// Documented default control-law constants. They are the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH
17	// for the tuning the simulation harness exercises; DefaultConfig assembles them.
18	// Every value is named and justified rather than appearing as a magic number in
19	// the algorithm.
20	const (
21		// DefaultDataShards is K, the fixed number of data shards per coding group the
22		// controller sizes parity for. Chosen to match a typical FEC group.
23		DefaultDataShards = 10
24	
25		// DefaultMaxParity caps M at 100% overhead (M == K): beyond a 1:1 parity ratio
26		// the marginal recovery per byte of overhead is poor, so the loop saturates
27		// there rather than spending unbounded bandwidth under pathological loss.
28		DefaultMaxParity = 10
29	
30		// DefaultAlpha is the EWMA weight of the newest loss sample. 0.3 keeps ~3-4
31		// samples of memory: heavy enough to reject single-sample telemetry noise, light
32		// enough to react to a genuine sustained shift within a few probe intervals.
33		DefaultAlpha = 0.3
34	
35		// DefaultRaiseThreshold is the smoothed-loss level (5%) at/above which the loop
36		// starts adding parity. Below it, occasional loss is cheaper to absorb via
37		// retransmit/latency than to mask with standing redundancy.
38		DefaultRaiseThreshold = 0.05
39	
40		// DefaultLowerThreshold is the smoothed-loss level (2%) at/below which the loop
41		// is eligible to shed parity. The gap to RaiseThreshold is the hysteresis
42		// deadband: loss wandering between 2% and 5% neither raises nor lowers M.
43		DefaultLowerThreshold = 0.02
44	
45		// DefaultSafetyFactor is the headroom over the mean loss the parity is sized to
46		// mask. 1.5 sizes each group to tolerate 1.5x the smoothed loss, covering the
47		// binomial variance by which an individual group exceeds the mean. It applies
48		// only when TargetResidual is unset (the legacy SafetyFactor sizing mode).
49		DefaultSafetyFactor = 1.5
50	
51		// DefaultMaxStep bounds |ΔM| per RateInterval. A single spike can move M by at
52		// most 2 parity shards per interval, so redundancy slews rather than jumps.
53		DefaultMaxStep = 2
54	
55		// DefaultRateInterval is the minimum wall time between two M changes. Combined
56		// with MaxStep it caps the redundancy slew rate at MaxStep shards per 500ms.
57		DefaultRateInterval = 500 * time.Millisecond
58	
59		// DefaultDwell is how long smoothed loss must stay at/below LowerThreshold before
60		// the first decrease. 3s of quiet is required to shed parity, so a brief lull in
61		// a lossy period does not prematurely strip protection (raise fast, lower slow).
62		DefaultDwell = 3 * time.Second
63	)
64	
65	// Config parameterizes the adaptive FEC controller. All fields are validated at
66	// construction (fail-fast): a mis-tuned band or an out-of-field parity bound is
67	// rejected by NewController rather than producing undefined control behavior at
68	// runtime.
69	type Config struct {
70		// DataShards is K, the fixed group size the controller sizes parity for. >= 1.
71		DataShards int
72		// MaxParity is the upper bound on the emitted M. >= 1 and DataShards+MaxParity
73		// <= MaxShards (the Reed-Solomon field limit).
74		MaxParity int
75		// Alpha is the EWMA weight of the newest loss sample, in (0,1]. Smaller = more
76		// smoothing.
77		Alpha float64
78		// RaiseThreshold is the smoothed-loss level at/above which M may rise. In
79		// (LowerThreshold, 1).
80		RaiseThreshold float64
81		// LowerThreshold is the smoothed-loss level at/below which M may fall. In
82		// [0, RaiseThreshold). The gap to RaiseThreshold is the hysteresis deadband.
83		LowerThreshold float64
84		// SafetyFactor is the headroom multiplier on the smoothed loss the parity is
85		// sized to mask. >= 1 in the SafetyFactor sizing mode (TargetResidual unset);
86		// MUST be 0 when TargetResidual is set (the two modes are mutually exclusive).
87		SafetyFactor float64
88		// TargetResidual, when set (> 0), selects the RESIDUAL-SLA sizing mode and is
89		// the PRIMARY parity surface: instead of the bare SafetyFactor multiplier the
90		// controller derives M by inverting the binomial residual model
91		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss), see residual.go) to the smallest
92		// M whose modeled residual is <= TargetResidual, clamped to [0, MaxParity]. It
93		// SUPERSEDES SafetyFactor (which must be 0 when this is set). Must be in (0,1)
94		// when set; 0 selects the legacy SafetyFactor path.
95		TargetResidual float64
96		// MaxStep bounds |ΔM| applied per RateInterval. >= 1.
97		MaxStep int
98		// RateInterval is the minimum time between two M changes. > 0.
99		RateInterval time.Duration
100		// Dwell is the minimum time smoothed loss must stay at/below LowerThreshold
101		// before the first decrease. >= 0.
102		Dwell time.Duration
103	}
104	
105	// DefaultConfig returns a Config populated from the documented default control
106	// constants.
107	func DefaultConfig() Config {
108		return Config{
109			DataShards:     DefaultDataShards,
110			MaxParity:      DefaultMaxParity,
111			Alpha:          DefaultAlpha,
112			RaiseThreshold: DefaultRaiseThreshold,
113			LowerThreshold: DefaultLowerThreshold,
114			SafetyFactor:   DefaultSafetyFactor,
115			MaxStep:        DefaultMaxStep,
116			RateInterval:   DefaultRateInterval,
117			Dwell:          DefaultDwell,
118		}
119	}
120	
121	// Validate enforces the control-law invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned
122	// controller is rejected at construction rather than misbehaving at runtime.
123	func (c Config) Validate() error {
124		if c.DataShards < 1 {
125			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: DataShards (K) must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
126		}
127		if c.MaxParity < 1 {
128			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: MaxParity must be >= 1, got %d", c.MaxParity)
129		}
130		if c.DataShards+c.MaxParity > MaxShards {
131			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: DataShards+MaxParity must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", MaxShards, c.DataShards+c.MaxParity)
132		}
133		if !(c.Alpha > 0 && c.Alpha <= 1) || math.IsNaN(c.Alpha) {
134			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: Alpha must be in (0,1], got %g", c.Alpha)
135		}
136		if math.IsNaN(c.LowerThreshold) || c.LowerThreshold < 0 {
137			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: LowerThreshold must be >= 0, got %g", c.LowerThreshold)
138		}
139		if math.IsNaN(c.RaiseThreshold) || c.RaiseThreshold >= 1 {
140			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: RaiseThreshold must be < 1, got %g", c.RaiseThreshold)
141		}
142		if c.LowerThreshold >= c.RaiseThreshold {
143			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: LowerThreshold (%g) must be < RaiseThreshold (%g) so the hysteresis deadband is non-empty", c.LowerThreshold, c.RaiseThreshold)
144		}
145		if math.IsNaN(c.TargetResidual) {
146			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: TargetResidual must be a number, got NaN")
147		}
148		if c.TargetResidual != 0 {
149			// Residual-SLA sizing mode: TargetResidual governs and SafetyFactor is inert,
150			// so the two must not both be set (mutually exclusive, fail-fast).
151			if c.TargetResidual < 0 || c.TargetResidual >= 1 {
152				return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: TargetResidual must be in (0,1) when set, got %g", c.TargetResidual)
153			}
154			if c.SafetyFactor != 0 {
155				return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: SafetyFactor and TargetResidual are mutually exclusive (TargetResidual is the primary residual-SLA surface); leave SafetyFactor 0 when TargetResidual is set, got SafetyFactor=%g", c.SafetyFactor)
156			}
157		} else if math.IsNaN(c.SafetyFactor) || math.IsInf(c.SafetyFactor, 0) || c.SafetyFactor < 1 {
158			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: SafetyFactor must be a finite value >= 1 (or set TargetResidual instead), got %g", c.SafetyFactor)
159		}
160		if c.MaxStep < 1 {
161			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: MaxStep must be >= 1, got %d", c.MaxStep)
162		}
163		if c.RateInterval <= 0 {
164			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: RateInterval must be > 0, got %s", c.RateInterval)
165		}
166		if c.Dwell < 0 {
167			return fmt.Errorf("adaptivefec: Dwell must be >= 0, got %s", c.Dwell)
168		}
169		return nil
170	}
```

```go
1	package sched
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
5	)
6	
7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.
12	const (
13		// PickNone means no path is currently eligible (a total outage among this
14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)
22	
23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.
82		Recompute()
83	}
84	
85	// PathQuality is the per-path measured-quality source the weighted scheduler reads
86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
87	// (RTT/Jitter/Loss), ANALOGOUS to PathHealth but exposing the quality snapshot
88	// rather than the up/down verdict. *telemetry.Prober satisfies BOTH PathHealth and
89	// PathQuality (its Estimate() is mutex-guarded, so it meets the same concurrency
90	// contract PathHealth documents), which is how one *Prober per path feeds liveness
91	// AND weight to the scheduler. The weighted scheduler's unit tests drive a synthetic
92	// PathQuality so the RTT/loss → weight formula is exercised on hand-built Estimates
93	// with no real probe stream.
94	type PathQuality interface {
95		Estimate() telemetry.Estimate
96	}
97	
98	// DynamicScheduler is a Scheduler whose path set can change at runtime (T30): a
99	// path may be admitted or dropped WITHOUT rebuilding the scheduler or disturbing
100	// the active selection of the surviving paths. The multipath Bind holds its
101	// scheduler as a plain Scheduler and type-asserts to this richer interface only
102	// when a runtime path add/remove is requested, so the Scheduler seam (and the
103	// T21 weighted policy that will implement it) is preserved: a scheduler that
104	// does not support dynamic membership simply cannot back a reloadable bind.
105	//
106	// The index space is the SAME priority-ordered index Pick returns, and the Bind
107	// keeps its own path slice index-aligned with this scheduler's path list, so an
108	// index returned by Pick always addresses the matching path. Both methods are
109	// safe for concurrent callers.
110	type DynamicScheduler interface {
111		Scheduler
112		// AddPath admits h as a NEW lowest-priority path (highest index) and returns
113		// that index. Appending at the tail cannot change any existing path's index
114		// nor steal the active selection from a higher-priority survivor: an
115		// active-backup policy only ever selects the new path once every
116		// higher-priority path is down. h must be non-nil.
117		AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error)
118		// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one)
119		// and repairs the cached selection so a surviving path keeps being selected
120		// and, if the dropped path was the active one, egress fails over to the best
121		// remaining path on the next Pick. i must be in range.
122		RemovePath(i int) error
123		// SetPaths replaces the ENTIRE path/health list with health (priority order,
124		// index 0 = preferred primary) and clears any cached selection, so the next
125		// Pick re-derives the active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
126		// every Open to re-align the scheduler's membership with the path slice it
127		// rebuilds from the current definitions — the single reconciliation point that
128		// makes a runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned
129		// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers; health must be non-empty and hold
130		// no nil element.
131		SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error
132	}
133	
134	// PathHealth is the per-path liveness the scheduler consumes: the read side of
135	// the T13 telemetry liveness state machine, so the scheduler reads exactly the
136	// up/down verdict T13 already computes (with its own probe-loss hysteresis)
137	// rather than duplicating detection here.
138	//
139	// Concurrency contract: Pick runs on the Bind send path CONCURRENTLY with the
140	// probe/timer goroutines that mutate liveness, so a production PathHealth MUST
141	// be INTERNALLY SYNCHRONIZED. *telemetry.Prober satisfies this — its State()
142	// takes the Prober's mutex (probe.go). A bare *telemetry.Liveness does NOT: it
143	// is not independently synchronized (probe.go: "must be reached only through
144	// the Prober") and its State() is an unguarded field read (liveness.go), so
145	// wiring a live, probe-driven *Liveness directly here is a data race — use a
146	// *Prober or an explicitly-locked adapter instead. (The sched unit tests drive
147	// a raw *Liveness on a single goroutine with no concurrent writer, which is not
148	// a race and is fine for testing.)
149	type PathHealth interface {
150		State() telemetry.PathState
151	}
152	
153	// AlwaysUp is a PathHealth that reports StateUp unconditionally. It is the
154	// composition-root placeholder used until the probe-transport wiring (a
155	// follow-up task) drives real per-path *telemetry.Prober liveness: with every
156	// path statically Up, the active-backup scheduler keeps all egress on the
157	// preferred primary (the data-thrift bring-up behaviour), and swapping in real
158	// Probers later activates failover with no further Bind or scheduler change.
159	type AlwaysUp struct{}
160	
161	// State reports the path as up.
162	func (AlwaysUp) State() telemetry.PathState { return telemetry.StateUp }
```

```go
85	// RecordEcho on every authenticated probe echo, and Tick on a periodic timer.
86	// It starts Down and requires UpAfterSuccesses echoes before declaring Up, so a
87	// path is never reported healthy before it has actually answered.
88	type Liveness struct {
89		cfg   LivenessConfig
90		clock Clock
91		log   log.Logger
92	
93		state      PathState
94		lastGood   time.Time
95		haveGood   bool
96		goodStreak int
97	}
98	
99	// NewLiveness builds a per-path liveness machine. The supplied logger is tagged
100	// with the path name so every transition record carries the per-path field.
101	func NewLiveness(pathName string, cfg LivenessConfig, clock Clock, logger log.Logger) *Liveness {
102		return &Liveness{
103			cfg:   cfg,
104			clock: clock,
105			log:   logger.Path(pathName),
106			state: StateDown,
107		}
108	}
109	
110	// RecordEcho registers a successful, authenticated probe echo as a liveness
111	// heartbeat. Only echoes that are CONSECUTIVE — no more than DownAfter apart —
112	// accumulate toward Up; an echo that arrives after a longer silence starts the
113	// streak over, so UpAfterSuccesses stray echoes spread across arbitrary silence
114	// never bring a Down path Up. Once UpAfterSuccesses consecutive heartbeats
115	// accumulate, a Down path transitions Up.
116	func (l *Liveness) RecordEcho() {
117		now := l.clock.Now()
118		if l.haveGood && now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter {
119			l.goodStreak = 0 // silence broke the run of consecutive echoes
120		}
121		l.lastGood = now
122		l.haveGood = true
123		l.goodStreak++
124		if l.state == StateDown && l.goodStreak >= l.cfg.UpAfterSuccesses {
125			l.transition(StateUp, now)
126		}
127	}
128	
129	// Tick re-evaluates liveness against the clock. An Up path with no heartbeat for
130	// longer than DownAfter transitions Down; a Down path that has gone silent past
131	// DownAfter has its partial up-streak reset, so recovery requires a fresh run of
132	// UpAfterSuccesses consecutive echoes rather than echoes accumulated before the
133	// silence. Call Tick at least as often as the probe interval so detection latency
134	// stays within DownAfter plus one interval.
135	func (l *Liveness) Tick() {
136		now := l.clock.Now()
137		if !l.haveGood {
138			return
139		}
140		stale := now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter
141		switch {
142		case l.state == StateUp && stale:
143			l.goodStreak = 0
144			l.transition(StateDown, now)
145		case l.state == StateDown && stale:
146			l.goodStreak = 0
147		}
148	}
149	
150	// State returns the current liveness verdict.
151	func (l *Liveness) State() PathState { return l.state }
152	
153	func (l *Liveness) transition(to PathState, now time.Time) {
154		from := l.state
155		l.state = to
156		l.log.Info("path liveness transition",
157			"from", from.String(),
158			"to", to.String(),
159			"silence_ms", now.Sub(l.lastGood).Milliseconds(),
160		)
161	}
```

```go
176	// is folded into the per-path loss estimate whether or not it is fresh (a
177	// duplicate is idempotent, a reorder fills its gap), but only a FRESH echo yields
178	// a new RTT sample and a liveness heartbeat.
179	func (p *Prober) HandleEcho(raw []byte) error {
180		f, err := frame.Decode(p.psk, raw)
181		if err != nil {
182			return err
183		}
184		probe, ok := f.(frame.Probe)
185		if !ok {
186			return fmt.Errorf("telemetry: expected probe echo, got frame kind %d", f.Kind())
187		}
188		if probe.PathID != p.pathID {
189			return ErrPathMismatch
190		}
191		// The SessionID is immutable after construction, so it is read without the lock.
192		// A genuine echo of one of THIS boot's probes always carries our own SessionID
193		// (we stamped it; the responder reflected it verbatim). A mismatch is a stale
194		// cross-boot echo or a replay of one — reject it before it can advance this
195		// boot's high-water or liveness.
196		if probe.SessionID != p.sessionID {
197			return ErrSessionMismatch
198		}
199	
200		p.mu.Lock()
201		defer p.mu.Unlock()
202	
203		// Loss accounting sees every genuine echo of this path: a duplicate re-marks an
204		// already-received slot (idempotent) and a reordered echo fills its gap.
205		p.est.ObserveProbeEcho(probe.ProbeSeq)
206	
207		if !p.guard.Accept(probe.ProbeSeq) {
208			return ErrReplay
209		}
210		// Record the responder's live issued challenge from this FRESH echo so our
211		// subsequent probes prove liveness and (after a restart) get us re-adopted. Only
212		// fresh echoes update it, so a replayed old echo can never roll the learned
213		// challenge backward and stall re-adoption.
214		p.learnedChallenge = probe.Challenge
215		rtt := p.clock.Now().Sub(time.Unix(0, probe.TimestampNanos))
216		if rtt < 0 {
217			// A negative sample can only come from clock skew between send and receive;
218			// clamp rather than poison the EWMA with a negative RTT.
219			rtt = 0
220		}
221		p.est.ObserveRTT(rtt)
222		p.live.RecordEcho()
223		return nil
224	}
225

227	// once per probe interval.

229		p.mu.Lock()
230		defer p.mu.Unlock()
231		p.live.Tick()
232	}
```

```go
7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.

14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)

23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8

33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota

37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
```
